title: Fermionic non-Gaussianity via Bell sampling: monotones and efficient quantum algorithms
abstract: Fermionic non-Gaussianity is an essential resource for unlocking the full computational power of fermionic quantum platforms. In this work we develop monotones and efficient quantum algorithms for fermionic non-Gaussianity, all built on the eigenvalue structure of the operator $Λ= \sum_{j=1}^{2n}γ_j\otimesγ_j$ defined on two copies of an $n$-mode fermionic state, accessible via Bell sampling. In particular, we introduce the \emph{bridge degree} of even pure states, a novel non-Gaussianity monotone defined as the largest eigenvalue sector of $Λ$ populated by two copies of the state. Our key technical result is that the bridge degree is non-increasing under post-selected Gaussian protocols, which yields no-go theorems for Gaussian conversion stronger than those obtainable from previously known monotones and shows that the resource theory of fermionic non-Gaussianity is irreversible in the exact-conversion setting. Beyond this, the bridge degree exhibits several further features: it (i) is easy to compute, (ii) is efficiently witnessed through Bell sampling, (iii) lower-bounds the non-Gaussian gate complexity of state preparation, (iv) controls the non-Gaussian gate complexity of producing quantum state designs, and (v) naturally extends to mixed states via the Choi--Jamiołkowski isomorphism. We further develop an approximate variant together with an efficiently measurable lower bound, yielding an experimentally certifiable lower bound on the non-Gaussian cost of approximately preparing any state, based directly on Bell-sampling data. Finally, the same eigenvalue structure underlies two Bell-sampling-based algorithmic primitives, both with polynomial sample complexity: a two-copy Gaussianity test with perfect completeness, optimal among two-copy tests sharing this property, and a test for the state $2$-design property of matchgate-invariant ensembles.
summary: This work develops a resource theory for fermionic non-Gaussianity using the 'bridge degree' monotone, derived from the eigenvalue structure of a specific operator accessible via Bell sampling. It proves this degree is irreversible under Gaussian operations, yielding strong lower bounds for tasks like state preparation. The paper culminates in efficient, sample-efficient quantum algorithms to test for Gaussianity and certify the 2-design property using this measure.
main_problem: The paper aims to quantify fermionic non-Gaussianity, which is a crucial resource for achieving quantum computational advantage beyond classical simulation capabilities.
main_result: They introduce the 'bridge degree' as a novel, computable, and irreversible non-Gaussianity monotone, establishing strong lower bounds on the non-Gaussian gate cost for state preparation and 2-design certification.
method: The analysis centers on the eigenvalue structure of the 'bridge operator' ($\Lambda$) acting on two copies of the fermionic state, which is accessible via Bell sampling measurements.
model_or_system: The system is based on fermionic quantum computation, utilizing fermionic Gaussian unitaries (matchgates) acting on $n$-mode fermionic states, often restricted to 'even' states.
key_observables: Bridge degree ($\Lambda_d(\psi)$), approximate bridge degree ($\Lambda_{d,\epsilon}(\psi)$), and the bridge spectral distribution ($p_\psi(\lambda)$).
important_parameters: The complexity scales are related to $n$ (number of modes), $\epsilon$ (approximation error), and $\delta$ (failure probability).
main_assumptions: The primary assumption is that the resource theory framework can be successfully mapped onto the eigenvalue structure of the bridge operator, and that Bell sampling provides an efficient measurement tool.
figures_summary: Not specified, but the notes reference TABLE I summarizing various non-Gaussianity monotones and their properties.
paper_structure: The paper develops the bridge degree monotone, proves its monotonicity under Gaussian protocols (establishing irreversibility), derives lower bounds for state preparation and 2-design costs, and finally presents efficient Bell-sampling algorithms for testing Gaussianity and 2-design properties.
why_interesting: It provides a concrete, measurable, and theoretically robust resource measure (the bridge degree) for fermionic quantum computation, directly linking structural properties to computational resource costs.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. A t-doped Gaussian protocol. Blue boxes E0, E1, . . . , Et denote arbitrary Gaussian protocols, possibly with post-selection; orange boxes W1, W2, . . . , Wt are non-Gaussian gates, each acting on two qubits (four Majorana modes) in arbitrary locations.
